summary:
High-risk regulatory changes for food seasoning/snack compliance include:
*   **Global (Codex):** New requirement to verify contaminant maximum levels.
*   **GCC/GSO:** Mandatory allergen declarations, general labelling (GSO 9:2022), Halal (GSO 2055-1:2015), and microbiological limits (GSO 1016:2015) all require verification.
*   **Japan:** Foundational Food Sanitation Law compliance is explicitly noted for all food safety aspects.
*   **Malaysia:** Allergen declarations under Food Regulation 1985 require verification.
*   **Singapore:** Prohibitions on non-permitted additives and contaminants exceeding limits are highlighted, along with mandatory declaration of all ingredients/additives. Specific veterinary drug residues are prohibited in meat/meat-derived products.
*   **Taiwan:** Mandatory declaration for 10 specific allergens and sulphite (>=10 mg/kg) is now required.
*   **United States:** New or reinforced requirements include: prohibition of delisted color additives and other prohibited substances; mandatory declaration of nine major food allergens (with specific formatting); an action level of 20 ppb for total aflatoxins; defined standards for 'gluten-free' claims; allergen preventive controls; and mandatory declaration of cochineal extract, carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents (>=10 ppm).

Additionally, several moderate-risk changes have been introduced across various regions (China, Codex, EU, GCC/GSO, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, Singapore), primarily adding new or updated requirements for: Additive Limits (specifications, permitted uses), Contaminant Limits (mycotoxins, agricultural chemicals, BSE, radioactivity, general contaminants), Labelling Requirements (export/import, food categories, health claims, nutrition labeling, sports foods, voluntary expiration dates), Microbiology Limits (criteria, specifications), and Salt/Sodium Limits. Some existing moderate-risk rules were also removed or modified, indicating potential updates or reclassification. All changes, especially those marked 'verification required', necessitate consulting the full regulatory documents for precise details and compliance.

changes.newLimit: Verification required against Codex maximum levels (MLs)
changes.notes: Maximum levels (MLs) for contaminants are established by the Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) and are legally permitted concentrations. Codex works to keep these levels as low as possible based on sound scientific evidence.

Mandatory allergen declaration requirements for seasoning, snack food, and processed foods are expected to be detailed within relevant GSO technical regulations, likely including GSO 9:2022, requiring verification of the full GSO standards database.

This technical regulation sets mandatory requirements for the labelling of prepackaged foodstuffs, which includes seasoning, snack food, and other processed foods. Specific details regarding warnings, ingredient lists, nutritional information, and allergen declarations require verification of the full document.
This technical regulation sets general labelling requirements for prepackaged foodstuffs. Specific details must be verified by consulting the full standard.
This technical regulation outlines general requirements for Halal food. Specific details must be verified by consulting the full standard.

This technical regulation establishes microbiological limits for foodstuffs intended for human consumption and food ingredients used in the food industry. Specific limits must be verified by consulting the full standard.
This foundational law governs all aspects of food safety, including additives, contaminants, microbiology, and labeling.
The provided text is a PDF stream and does not contain directly readable content. Food Regulations 1985, as a comprehensive food law, would mandate allergen declarations for specified allergens.
Food products containing non-permitted additives cannot be imported or sold. An application can be submitted to SFA for evaluation.
Food products exceeding regulatory limits for contaminants or incidental constituents must not be imported or sold.
Import or sale of meat and food derived from meat containing diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol residues is prohibited.
All ingredients and additives in prepacked foods must be declared on the food label.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.newLimit: Mandatory declaration if present as ingredient or food additive.
changes.notes: These are 10 of the 11 mandatory allergens.
Mandatory declaration if concentration is 10 mg/kg or more.

changes.newLimit: Not permitted for use in food.
changes.notes: Delisted color additives are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30) and the Substances Added to Food inventory.
Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189) and the Substances Added to Food inventory.
The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
This is an Action Level. Specific action levels also exist for Brazil nuts, peanuts and peanut products, and pistachio nuts.

changes.newLimit: Must meet defined standard for gluten content.
changes.notes: The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling. Specific compliance requirements exist for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.
Establishments must implement written procedures to control allergen cross-contact and ensure accurate allergen labeling, as per the CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117).
Must be declared on the label as they are identified as allergenic substances.

The food source of a major food allergen must appear either in parentheses following the ingredient name or immediately after/next to the ingredient list in a "contains" statement. The specific type of tree nut, species of fish, and species of Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
Required to be identified on labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
Must be declared on food labels when present at or above 10 ppm total sulfites due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals.
Specific additive limits for various food categories, including seasonings, snack foods, and processed foods, must be verified by consulting the full GB 2760-2015 standard document.

The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used. Users are encouraged to consult the Preamble when using this database.
